response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Stereotype threat is basically when you're aware of negative stereotypes about your group in a certain area, and that awareness messes with your performance. Like, if you're taking a math test and you know there's this stereotype that immigrants or working-class people aren't good at math, that stress alone can actually make you perform worse, even if you're capable. It's not just in your head either—the anxiety is real and it affects your brain's ability to focus and think clearly. I've seen this happen around me in Chicago, honestly. People who are super smart but don't pursue STEM fields because they've internalized these ideas that they don't belong there. It's frustrating because talent gets wasted. I think we absolutely should address it. My dad has a PhD and my mom didn't finish higher education, so I've seen both sides of the educational spectrum in my own home. I know how different the expectations and encouragement can feel depending on your background. In STEM specifically, women, minorities, and kids from working-class families often face this pressure, and it's holding back a lot of potential. How do we fix it? I think it starts with making STEM spaces feel more inclusive and welcoming. Schools and universities could do more to highlight role models from different backgrounds—show kids that people like them succeed in these fields. Teachers matter too; they need to believe in their students and communicate that intelligence isn't fixed, that struggle is part of learning. Companies hiring for tech and science roles should actively work against their own biases and create cultures where diverse people feel valued. On a personal level, I think building confidence through mentorship and community support helps a lot. When you're around people who look like you and have succeeded in STEM, it changes your whole perspective about what's possible. It's not just about fixing individuals either; it's about changing the systems and cultures that create these stereotypes in the first place. Everyone benefits when we get more diverse minds working on scientific and technical problems.
